comments.rawMarkdown:
***[2026-05-14 Update]:*** This exploit (along with another reported by @pavelsavchenkov) have been fixed.  Details of both issues are documented in `neurogolf_utils.py`.  Thanks all — until next time!

---

Wonderful, many thanks for the detailed bug report!  Fixes should be coming today — it's a very surgical change, so most teams need not worry.

> What's the rule if a scorer bug is never publicly disclosed but is quietly exploited until the end of the contest?

If a team were to discover & hold onto such a vulnerability until the very end, we reserve the right to disqualify such participants, as per sections 3.8.d and 3.8.e of our [rules](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/neurogolf-2026/rules).  However, we encourage teams to continue to hunt for such issues and report them, as the hardening of the metric ought to help everyone in the long run.

comments.rawMarkdown:
Thanks for reporting!

I'm currently low on limits, so not running the main optimization loop, but managed to find the exploit quite quickly.

It scores `25.0` on all tasks except two.

On the positive side, the exploit should be fairly easy to close.

firstMessage.rawMarkdown:
I've found a scoring bug that can significantly reduce memory cost. For task069, this bug can reduce mem cost calculated by scorer from `44206` to `625`, and raise points from `14.30` to `18.47`. I've already gotten about +50 points from a fairly minimal automated version of this. Rewriting all 400 tasks that fit the exploit well probably can get  +400 to +1000 points. 

I've already submit details to host @mmoffitt. But I think the scorer is still not perfect after fix this bug. One thing I'd like the organizers to clarify: what's the rule if a scorer bug is never publicly disclosed but is quietly exploited until the end of the contest? Are submissions that depend on it disqualified retroactively, or do they stand?
